summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically recommends 'The Typing of the Dead,' a game that transforms typing practice into a zombie-shooting experience. He argues that software developers should be competent typists and presents this quirky game as both an effective typing tutor and entertaining multiplayer experience. The post celebrates the game's bizarre combination of educational software and B-movie horror aesthetics. Atwood also notes the interesting trivia that Mavis Beacon, the iconic typing tutor character, was never a real person.
key_points: Software developers should prioritize being decent typists as a fundamental skill
The Typing of the Dead successfully combines education with entertainment in an absurdly creative way
The game offers competitive 2-player network play with detailed statistics tracking
Mavis Beacon is a fictional marketing creation, similar to other tech mascots like Carmen Sandiego
money_quotes: Any self-respecting software developer should be a decent typist.
Typing of the Dead is like Mavis Beacon Teaches Typing... if Mavis Beacon was a flesh-eating zombie!

It's a challenging, remarkably well thought out typing tutor, a tongue-in-cheek b-grade zombie movie, and the most hilarious multiplayer game experience you'll probably ever have – all at the same time.
It's hard to explain just how wondrously bizarre Typing of the Dead is.
Mavis Beacon isn't a real person. She's an imaginary identity created entirely for the purpose of marketing.
key_insight: The best way to improve a fundamental developer skill like typing is to make practice genuinely fun, even if that means fighting zombies.
